title: Androgenetic Alopecia: Male Pattern Baldness
content:
Overview

Androgenetic alopecia (AGA), commonly known as male pattern baldness, is the most prevalent form of hair loss in men, affecting approximately 50% of males by age 50. This progressive condition results from a combination of genetic predisposition and sensitivity to dihydrotestosterone (DHT), a hormone derived from testosterone.

Pathophysiology

In genetically susceptible individuals, hair follicles in the scalp contain increased numbers of androgen receptors. These follicles undergo miniaturization when exposed to DHT, a process wherein the hair shaft becomes progressively thinner and shorter. The growth phase (anagen) shortens while the resting phase (telogen) lengthens, resulting in visible hair loss.

Clinical Presentation

Male pattern baldness typically presents with:

  • Receding hairline (bitemporal recession)
  • Crown thinning or balding
  • Progressive loss over months to years
  • Preserved occipital and temporal hair
  • No associated scalp inflammation or symptoms

Diagnosis

Diagnosis is primarily clinical, based on hair loss pattern and distribution. The Norwood-Hamilton scale categorizes severity from Type I (minimal recession) to Type VII (extensive baldness). Dermoscopy may reveal decreased hair density and hair shaft diameter variation. Scalp biopsy is rarely necessary unless atypical features are present.

Treatment Options

Topical minoxidil and oral finasteride are FDA-approved treatments that can slow progression and promote regrowth. Hair transplantation offers definitive restoration, while low-level laser therapy and platelet-rich plasma treatments show promise in preliminary studies. Early intervention yields superior outcomes.

Prognosis

Without treatment, androgenetic alopecia typically progresses slowly. Approximately 25% of men experience noticeable hair loss by age 30, and this increases with advancing age. Early recognition and treatment initiation provide the best opportunity for hair preservation.
meta_description: Male pattern baldness overview, causes, Norwood scale classification, and FDA-approved treatments including minoxidil and finasteride.

title: Female Pattern Hair Loss (Androgenetic Alopecia in Women)
content:
Overview

Female pattern hair loss (FPHL) is a non-scarring alopecia affecting women of all ages, though incidence increases significantly after age 50. It accounts for up to 40% of female hair loss complaints and shares similar pathophysiology to male pattern baldness, involving DHT sensitivity in genetically predisposed individuals.

Epidemiology and Risk Factors

FPHL affects approximately 5-10% of women before age 50, increasing to 40-50% by age 80. Risk factors include family history, elevated androgen levels, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), hormonal fluctuations, and certain medications. Unlike males, women rarely develop complete baldness.

Clinical Features

Female pattern hair loss characteristically presents with:

  • Diffuse hair thinning over the crown and vertex
  • Preserved frontal hairline (unlike male pattern baldness)
  • Widening central hair part
  • Increased shedding when shampooing or brushing
  • Progressive reduction in hair density
  • No scalp inflammation or tenderness

Classification

The Ludwig scale categorizes female pattern hair loss from Type I (minimal widening of center part) to Type III (significant thinning with exposed scalp). The Savin scale provides additional assessment of hair density at crown and frontal regions.

Diagnostic Approach

Clinical examination combined with hair pull test and dermoscopy typically establishes diagnosis. Hormonal evaluation including androgen levels, PCOS screening, and thyroid function testing may be indicated when presenting features suggest endocrinopathy. Scalp biopsy is rarely necessary for uncomplicated cases.

Treatment Modalities

Topical minoxidil is FDA-approved for female pattern hair loss and generally demonstrates better tolerance than oral medications. Oral finasteride, while effective, carries teratogenic risks and is typically reserved for postmenopausal women. Anti-androgens such as spironolactone may benefit women with elevated androgens. Hair transplantation provides long-term restoration.

Management Considerations

Early intervention offers superior outcomes, and combination therapies often provide enhanced benefits. Counseling regarding realistic expectations, treatment duration (3-6 months before visible improvement), and long-term maintenance is essential for patient satisfaction.
meta_description: Female pattern hair loss causes, Ludwig scale classification, minoxidil and spironolactone treatments, and management strategies.

title: Alopecia Areata: Autoimmune Hair Loss
content:
Overview

Alopecia areata (AA) is a common non-scarring alopecia characterized by sudden onset of hair loss in well-demarcated patches. It affects approximately 1-2% of the population and results from autoimmune dysfunction targeting hair follicles. Onset can occur at any age, though peak incidence occurs in the second and third decades of life.

Pathophysiology

In alopecia areata, CD8+ T lymphocytes and other immune cells infiltrate hair follicles in the anagen growth phase, leading to premature telogen conversion and hair shedding. Genetic susceptibility and environmental triggers (stress, infections, trauma) likely contribute to disease initiation. Association with other autoimmune conditions including thyroid disease, vitiligo, and systemic lupus erythematosus is well established.

Clinical Presentation

Typical features include:

  • Sudden onset of well-circumscribed alopecic patches
  • Non-scarring alopecia with preserved follicular openings
  • Exclamation mark hairs (fractured hairs with dark tips and pale roots) at patch margins
  • Nail pitting or ridging in 10-20% of cases
  • Possible beard, eyebrow, or body hair involvement
  • Pruritus or paresthesia preceding hair loss

Severity Classification

Disease extent guides prognosis and treatment decisions. Localized patches affecting less than 25% of scalp show better prognosis, while extensive alopecia totalis and alopecia universalis indicate more severe disease with lower remission rates.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is primarily clinical. Dermoscopy reveals typical features including empty follicles, acute phase inflammation, and structural hair changes. Scalp biopsy, though rarely necessary, demonstrates characteristic lymphocytic infiltration around anagen follicles with preserved sebaceous glands and arrector pili muscles.

Treatment Options

Corticosteroid injections and topical corticosteroids provide first-line therapy for localized disease. Systemic corticosteroids may be considered for extensive involvement. Topical immunotherapy (dinitrochlorobenzene or squaric acid dibutyl ester), minoxidil, and newer JAK inhibitors show promise. Psychological support is essential given the significant emotional impact.

Prognosis

Approximately 50% of patients with localized alopecia areata experience spontaneous remission within 1 year. Negative prognostic factors include early age of onset, extensive alopecia, and associated autoimmune conditions. Long-standing disease with multiple episodes carries lower remission likelihood.
meta_description: Alopecia areata pathophysiology, exclamation mark hairs, immunotherapy and corticosteroid treatments, prognosis and remission rates.

title: Alopecia Totalis and Alopecia Universalis
content:
Overview

Alopecia totalis (AT) and alopecia universalis (AU) represent severe extensions of alopecia areata, characterized by complete loss of scalp hair (AT) or all body hair including scalp, facial, and body hair (AU). These conditions affect approximately 5% of alopecia areata patients and carry significant psychological implications.

Distinction and Definitions

Alopecia totalis involves complete loss of scalp hair while preserving body, facial, and pubic hair. Alopecia universalis extends to complete hair loss across the entire body, including eyebrows, eyelashes, and all body hair. Both conditions result from the same autoimmune mechanism as alopecia areata but affecting a substantially greater percentage of hair follicles.

Clinical Presentation

Patients present with:

  • Complete scalp alopecia (AT and AU)
  • Loss of eyebrows and eyelashes (AU)
  • Absence of all body and facial hair (AU)
  • Smooth scalp without inflammation or scarring
  • Preserved scalp sensitivity and normal skin texture
  • Potential associated autoimmune conditions

Psychological Impact

The extensive nature of hair loss in these conditions creates substantial psychological burden, affecting self-esteem, social interaction, and quality of life. Depression and anxiety are common comorbidities. Comprehensive psychological support and counseling are essential components of management.

Diagnostic Considerations

Diagnosis is typically clinical. Dermoscopy of affected areas reveals absence of hair follicles. Investigation for associated autoimmune conditions including thyroid disease, vitiligo, and other manifestations is warranted given increased prevalence of systemic autoimmunity.

Treatment Approaches

Systemic corticosteroids are often employed for extensive disease, though response rates remain variable. JAK inhibitors, including ruxolitinib, show promising results in clinical trials for severe alopecia areata variants. Topical immunotherapy and minoxidil provide supportive benefits. Realistic expectations regarding treatment outcomes are important given the extensive hair loss.

Supportive Care

Wigs, hairpieces, eyebrow reconstruction, and eyelash prosthetics provide essential cosmetic support. Support groups and mental health counseling address the significant psychosocial impact. Sun protection for the exposed scalp is important to prevent carcinoma development.
meta_description: Alopecia totalis and universalis pathology, psychological impact, JAK inhibitors and corticosteroid therapy, supportive care options.

title: Telogen Effluvium: Diffuse Hair Shedding
content:
Overview

Telogen effluvium is a reversible, non-scarring alopecia characterized by synchronized shift of hair follicles from the growth phase (anagen) to the resting phase (telogen), resulting in diffuse hair shedding. It typically affects 10-20% of the hair follicle population and is one of the most common causes of hair loss in dermatology practice.

Pathophysiology

A significant physiologic stress triggers premature termination of anagen phase, shifting follicles into telogen prematurely. After 2-3 months in telogen phase, these hairs are shed, resulting in sudden onset of increased hair loss 2-4 weeks after the triggering event. The condition is self-limited, with spontaneous resolution as the follicle cycle normalizes.

Triggering Factors

Common precipitants include:

  • Fever or acute systemic illness
  • Severe emotional or psychological stress
  • Major surgery or anesthesia
  • Significant weight loss or nutritional deficiency
  • Medications (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, anticoagulants)
  • Hormonal changes (postpartum, oral contraceptive initiation)
  • Chronic illness including thyroid dysfunction and lupus

Clinical Features

Patients report sudden onset of increased hair shedding, often noticed during shampooing or brushing. Examination reveals diffuse hair thinning without alopecic patches. Hair pull test typically yields 3 or more telogen hairs (compared to normal 0-1). Scalp appears normal without inflammation or scarring.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is primarily clinical, supported by history of recent stressor and diffuse shedding pattern. Dermoscopy demonstrates telogen hairs with preserved hair follicles. Scalp biopsy is rarely necessary but would reveal increased telogen phase hairs (>25% of follicles in telogen) with normal sebaceous gland structure.

Acute vs. Chronic Telogen Effluvium

Acute telogen effluvium typically resolves within 3-6 months of the triggering event. Chronic telogen effluvium persists beyond 6 months without identifiable trigger, affecting middle-aged women predominantly. Some patients develop persistent shedding despite treatment.

Management

Identifying and addressing the underlying trigger is paramount. Treatment is largely supportive, with reassurance regarding the benign and self-limiting nature of the condition. Nutritional supplementation (iron, zinc, B vitamins) may benefit patients with documented deficiencies. Minoxidil provides supportive benefit in chronic cases.
meta_description: Telogen effluvium causes, acute vs. chronic forms, hair pull test findings, and management of diffuse hair shedding.

title: Traction Alopecia: Hair Loss from Physical Tension
content:
Overview

Traction alopecia is a form of acquired non-scarring to scarring alopecia resulting from chronic mechanical tension on hair follicles. It occurs predominantly in individuals who style hair tightly using braids, weaves, cornrows, buns, or extensions. Early recognition and modification of hair styling practices can prevent progression to permanent scarring alopecia.

Pathophysiology

Sustained mechanical traction on hair follicles causes both temporary disruption of the hair growth cycle and chronic inflammation of the follicle and surrounding tissue. Initially, the condition is non-scarring and reversible with cessation of the inciting tension. However, prolonged traction leads to fibrosis, inflammation, and permanent destruction of hair follicles, resulting in irreversible scarring alopecia.

Clinical Presentation

Early traction alopecia presents with:

  • Hair loss in distribution corresponding to tension sites (frontotemporal, marginal areas)
  • Follicular hyperkeratosis or papules at affected margins
  • Possible pruritus or tenderness
  • Preserved hair follicles in early disease
  • History of tight hairstyling practices

Chronic traction alopecia demonstrates scarring with follicular loss and smooth scalp appearance.

Risk Factors

African-American females represent the highest-risk population due to cultural hair styling practices involving tight braiding and extensions. However, traction alopecia occurs across all ethnicities. Duration and frequency of traction, hair texture, and individual follicle sensitivity influence disease severity.

Diagnostic Assessment

Diagnosis is clinical, supported by characteristic alopecia pattern and history of tight hairstyling. Dermoscopy may reveal reduced hair density with increased follicular spacing. Scalp biopsy in chronic traction alopecia demonstrates inflammatory infiltration and follicular fibrosis.

Management and Prevention

Early cessation of tension-inducing hairstyles is critical to prevent irreversible scarring. Hair styling modifications including looser braiding, avoiding extensions, and varying hair styles should be implemented. Topical corticosteroids may reduce inflammation and associated symptoms. Once scarring has occurred, hair transplantation offers the only option for hair restoration.

Prognosis

Early-stage traction alopecia responds well to hairstyle modification, with potential for complete hair regrowth within months to years. Chronic, scarring traction alopecia carries poor prognosis for spontaneous recovery, necessitating surgical intervention for restoration.
meta_description: Traction alopecia pathophysiology, braids and hairstyle risks, clinical presentation, prevention strategies, and scarring progression.

title: Frontal Fibrosing Alopecia
content:
Overview

Frontal fibrosing alopecia (FFA) is a progressive primary scarring alopecia characterized by fibrosis and destruction of hair follicles along the hairline, with characteristic loss of the marginal row of hair follicles. It primarily affects middle-aged and postmenopausal women, though cases in men and younger individuals have been reported. The incidence appears to be increasing, particularly in Caucasian populations.

Pathophysiology

FFA results from chronic lymphocytic inflammation targeting hair follicles and sebaceous glands in the frontal scalp region. The disease progresses with follicular fibrosis and permanent loss of hair-bearing tissue. The etiology remains poorly understood, though genetic predisposition, hormonal factors, and environmental triggers may contribute. The uniform anterior-to-posterior progression pattern distinguishes FFA from other forms of scarring alopecia.

Clinical Features

Characteristic presentation includes:

  • Gradual recession of the hairline with well-demarcated border
  • Loss of the marginal row of follicles (distinguishing feature)
  • Band-like scarring alopecia of the frontal scalp
  • Preservation of behind-the-hairline follicles
  • Associated eyebrow loss in approximately 40% of cases
  • Associated facial papules and follicular hyperkeratosis
  • Pruritus or paresthesia at affected margins

Associated Features

Some patients develop concurrent lichen planopilaris on the scalp or lichen sclerosus of genital areas. Facial involvement with lichen planus or discoid lupus erythematosus may also occur. Systemic involvement is uncommon.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is clinical, based on characteristic hairline recession pattern and loss of marginal row follicles. Dermoscopy demonstrates reduced follicle density, follicular keratinization, and absent marginal follicles. Scalp biopsy shows lymphocytic infiltration with follicular fibrosis and sebaceous gland loss, features diagnostic of scarring alopecia.

Treatment Options

Early intervention is critical to slow disease progression. First-line treatments include topical and intralesional corticosteroids to reduce inflammation. Oral antiandrogens (spironolactone), 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride), and antimalarial agents (hydroxychloroquine) may benefit some patients. Newer JAK inhibitors show promise in preliminary reports. Advanced scarring alopecia may require surgical hair transplantation.

Prognosis

FFA is progressive, though rate of progression varies among patients. Early recognition and treatment may slow or stabilize disease. Once significant fibrosis occurs, medical therapy offers limited benefit, and surgical intervention becomes necessary.
meta_description: Frontal fibrosing alopecia in postmenopausal women, marginal row loss, corticosteroid and antiandrogen therapy, prognosis.

title: Lichen Planopilaris: Inflammatory Scarring Alopecia
content:
Overview

Lichen planopilaris (LPP) is a primary scarring alopecia characterized by lichen planus affecting the hair follicles and scalp. It represents the scarring variant of cutaneous lichen planus and can result in permanent hair loss if inadequately treated. The condition affects both adults and children, though peak incidence occurs in middle age with female predominance.

Pathophysiology

Lichen planopilaris results from a T-cell mediated autoimmune attack on the hair follicle, particularly targeting the outer root sheath. Chronic inflammation leads to fibrosis of the follicle and sebaceous gland destruction, ultimately resulting in permanent alopecia. The etiology remains incompletely understood, though genetic predisposition and possible triggers (infections, drugs, trauma) may be involved.

Clinical Presentation

Patients present with:

  • Slowly progressive hair loss in patches or diffuse distribution
  • Violaceous or erythematous inflammation at active margins
  • Follicular hyperkeratosis and papules
  • Pruritus, burning, or pain at affected areas
  • Possible associated lichen planus on skin or mucous membranes
  • Advanced disease shows scarring alopecia with smooth scalp
  • Peripilar inflammation with perifollicular involvement

Diagnostic Approach

Scalp biopsy is essential for diagnosis, revealing lymphocytic infiltration at the follicular bulge and surrounding tissue with sebaceous gland involvement. Distinctive features include circumscribed interface dermatitis and fibrosis in chronic lesions. Associated skin or mucosal lichen planus may be present.

Disease Classification

Lichen planopilaris is classified as primary scarring alopecia to distinguish it from secondary scarring alopecias. Variants include localized patches or more extensive involvement. Some patients develop coexistent frontal fibrosing alopecia (Graham-Little-Lassueur variant).

Management

Early intervention is paramount to prevent irreversible scarring. Intralesional and topical corticosteroids provide first-line therapy. Systemic corticosteroids may benefit extensive disease. Antimalarial agents (hydroxychloroquine), retinoids, and JAK inhibitors show promise in treatment-resistant cases. Minoxidil provides supportive benefit. Advanced scarring disease requires surgical hair transplantation.

Prognosis and Outcomes

Prognosis depends on disease stage at treatment initiation and treatment response. Early-stage disease with active inflammation responds better to medical therapy. Chronic disease with established scarring carries poor prognosis for medical management and may require surgical intervention.
meta_description: Lichen planopilaris scarring alopecia, autoimmune pathophysiology, scalp biopsy findings, corticosteroid and antimalarial treatment.

title: Central Centrifugal Cicatricial Alopecia
content:
Overview

Central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia (CCCA) is a primary scarring alopecia characterized by progressive hair loss beginning at the vertex or crown with centrifugal expansion. It predominantly affects African-American women and represents one of the most common forms of cicatricial alopecia in this population. Early recognition and intervention are essential to halt disease progression.

Pathophysiology

The exact mechanism of CCCA remains unclear, though follicular obstruction, inflammation, and premature follicle destruction appear central to pathogenesis. Some evidence suggests a genetic component combined with environmental triggers including chemical treatments, mechanical trauma from hairstyling, and thermal injury. Chronic inflammation leads to fibrosis and irreversible follicle destruction.

Clinical Presentation

CCCA typically manifests with:

  • Gradual onset of hair loss at the vertex or crown
  • Centrifugal expansion of alopecic area
  • Well-defined border between affected and unaffected areas
  • Smooth scalp surface with loss of follicular markings
  • Possible preceding erythema or inflammation at active margins
  • Typically affects the crown region but can progress to involve more scalp
  • Minimal symptoms though pruritus may occur

Clinical Subtypes

CCCA is classified as primary scarring alopecia. Some authors propose subtypes based on histologic findings (follicular degeneration, fibrosis patterns), though clinical utility of subtyping remains debated.

Diagnostic Assessment

Scalp biopsy is often performed to confirm diagnosis and assess disease stage. Histopathology reveals variable findings including follicular disruption, fibrosis, and inflammatory infiltration. Early lesions may show follicular obstruction with minimal fibrosis, while advanced disease demonstrates scarring with follicular loss.

Hairstyle Factors

Tight hairstyling practices and chemical hair treatments appear to exacerbate disease in predisposed individuals. However, CCCA occurs even in patients practicing loose styling, suggesting intrinsic follicular vulnerability beyond mechanical factors alone.

Treatment Approach

Early medical intervention slows disease progression. Topical and intralesional corticosteroids provide first-line therapy. Systemic agents including oral corticosteroids, antimalarials, and tetracycline antibiotics show benefit in some patients. JAK inhibitors represent emerging therapy. Once advanced scarring occurs, surgical intervention including hair transplantation provides the only option for hair restoration.

Prognosis

CCCA is progressive without treatment, though rate of progression varies. Early recognition and aggressive intervention may stabilize or slow disease. Established scarring carries poor prognosis for medical management.
meta_description: Central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia in African-American women, vertex hair loss, scalp biopsy, corticosteroid therapy.

title: Folliculitis Decalvans: Purulent Scarring Alopecia
content:
Overview

Folliculitis decalvans (FD) is a rare primary scarring alopecia characterized by recurrent pustular folliculitis with subsequent scarring and permanent hair loss. It typically affects middle-aged and older individuals of both sexes, though male predominance has been reported. The condition is often refractory to treatment and requires aggressive early intervention to prevent extensive hair loss.

Pathophysiology

The etiology of folliculitis decalvans remains incompletely understood. Theories include primary bacterial infection (particularly Staphylococcus aureus), follicular obstruction with secondary infection, and underlying autoimmune dysfunction targeting the follicle. The condition is not truly infectious, as antibiotics alone rarely produce sustained remission, suggesting an underlying follicular or immune disorder predisposing to recurrent pustule formation.

Clinical Features

Typical presentation includes:

  • Recurrent crops of follicular pustules on the scalp
  • Central hair loss with surrounding erythema
  • Purulent drainage from affected follicles
  • Pruritus or pain at affected sites
  • Progressive scarring alopecia with polygonal configuration
  • Boggy, inflamed scalp appearance in early disease
  • Smooth scarring alopecia as disease progresses
  • Possible bacterial colonization (commonly Staphylococcus aureus)

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is based on clinical presentation and confirmed by scalp biopsy. Histopathology demonstrates suppurative folliculitis with prominent neutrophil infiltration and follicular destruction. Bacterial cultures may yield growth of Staphylococcus aureus or other organisms, though positive culture does not establish primary infection.

Associated Conditions

Folliculitis decalvans may rarely be associated with dissecting cellulitis or acne keloidalis nuchae, conditions that similarly affect the scalp with pustulation and scarring.

Management

Early aggressive treatment is essential to prevent extensive scarring. Intralesional corticosteroids address inflammation directly. Systemic antibiotics (oral clindamycin or doxycycline) may suppress bacterial colonization. Systemic corticosteroids benefit some patients with extensive disease. Oral retinoids and JAK inhibitors show promise. Zinc supplementation has shown benefit in anecdotal reports. Elimination of secondary factors including poor hygiene or follicular occlusion aids treatment success.

Prognosis

Folliculitis decalvans is chronic and often difficult to treat. Without aggressive early intervention, progressive scarring and alopecia result. Some patients achieve remission with sustained therapy, while others require ongoing treatment to suppress disease activity.
meta_description: Folliculitis decalvans purulent scarring alopecia, Staphylococcal colonization, scalp biopsy, antibiotics and corticosteroid therapy.

title: Scalp Psoriasis: Diagnosis and Management
content:
Overview

Psoriasis affecting the scalp is one of the most common manifestations of this chronic inflammatory condition, affecting up to 80% of patients with cutaneous psoriasis at some point during their disease course. Scalp psoriasis often proves particularly resistant to treatment and significantly impacts quality of life due to visible scaling and pruritus.

Pathophysiology

Scalp psoriasis results from the same pathophysiologic mechanisms as cutaneous psoriasis elsewhere, involving dysregulation of innate and adaptive immunity with T-cell activation and increased keratinocyte proliferation. The scalp environment, with increased sebaceous gland activity and occlusion from hair, may predispose to more severe and treatment-resistant disease.

Clinical Presentation

Scalp psoriasis manifests with:

  • Erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale
  • Well-demarcated lesions extending to the hairline and neck
  • Moderate to severe pruritus
  • Possible hair loss (non-scarring) secondary to inflammation
  • Scale crusting and sealing of follicles
  • Variable severity from mild scaling to severe plaques
  • Lesions may extend beyond the visible hairline

Classification

Scalp psoriasis is classified as mild, moderate, or severe based on extent and pruritus severity. Nail involvement occurs in 10-15% of patients with scalp psoriasis. Associated systemic features including joint involvement should be assessed.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is primarily clinical, though biopsy may be performed if diagnosis is uncertain. Histopathology reveals parakeratosis, regular acanthosis, elongated rete ridges, and dilated vessels characteristic of psoriasis. Dermoscopy aids in clinical diagnosis.

First-Line Treatments

Topical corticosteroids remain first-line therapy, though scalp penetration is challenging due to hair coverage. High-potency formulations and occlusion overnight enhance efficacy. Coal tar and salicylic acid shampoos provide adjunctive benefit by promoting scale removal and improving topical medication penetration.

Second-Line and Advanced Therapies

Calcipotriene (vitamin D analog), tacrolimus, or combination therapies enhance outcomes. For extensive or treatment-resistant disease, systemic agents including methotrexate, acitretin, biologic agents (TNF-alpha inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors, IL-23 inhibitors), and JAK inhibitors provide effective options.

Management Strategies

Optimal management often requires combination therapy. Regular shampooing with gentle technique, avoiding hair trauma, and protective styling aid treatment success. Patient education regarding chronic disease nature and realistic expectations is essential.
meta_description: Scalp psoriasis treatment with topical steroids, vitamin D analogs, systemic biologics, and combination therapy approaches.

title: Seborrheic Dermatitis of the Scalp
content:
Overview

Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis that commonly affects the scalp, presenting as erythema and scaling typically in sebaceous gland-rich areas. It affects up to 5% of the general population, with higher prevalence in males, older adults, and individuals with neurologic conditions such as Parkinson's disease. The condition is often associated with Malassezia species colonization.

Pathophysiology

While Malassezia species are part of normal scalp flora, they may trigger inflammatory responses in predisposed individuals through lipid metabolism and immune activation. Sebaceous gland dysfunction, increased sebum production, and epidermal barrier impairment contribute to disease pathogenesis. Genetic factors and neurologic conditions increase susceptibility.

Clinical Presentation

Seborrheic dermatitis of the scalp presents with:

  • Erythema and yellow or brown scale adherent to scalp
  • Pruritus and possible burning sensation
  • Greasy appearance of scale
  • Distribution in sebaceous gland-rich areas (vertex, frontoparietal)
  • Often worse in winter or during stress
  • Variable severity from subtle scaling to significant inflammation
  • Possible exudative crusting in severe cases

Associated Features

Seborrheic dermatitis commonly affects other areas including eyebrows, nasolabial folds, and retroauricular areas. Higher prevalence exists in patients with HIV/AIDS, Parkinson's disease, and history of depression.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is primarily clinical. Dermoscopy may reveal follicular plugging and erythema. Biopsy, when performed, demonstrates spongiosis, acanthosis, and follicular involvement with preserved dermal-epidermal junction architecture.

First-Line Management

Medicated shampoos containing zinc pyrithione, ketoconazole, or selenium sulfide effectively reduce Malassezia colonization and provide symptomatic relief. Coal tar shampoos have traditional use. These should be applied 2-3 times weekly for optimal effect.

Topical Medications

Topical corticosteroids (low to medium potency) reduce inflammation and pruritus. Topical azoles (ketoconazole, clotrimazole) target Malassezia species. Combination approaches often provide superior outcomes. Tacrolimus offers steroid-sparing alternative for long-term management.

Maintenance and Prognosis

Seborrheic dermatitis is chronic and requires maintenance therapy to prevent recurrence. Most patients respond well to treatment, though complete resolution may not occur. Seasonal variation and relapse during stress periods are common.
meta_description: Seborrheic dermatitis scalp causes, Malassezia colonization, zinc pyrithione shampoos, ketoconazole, topical steroids.

title: Scalp Folliculitis: Bacterial and Fungal Infection
content:
Overview

Scalp folliculitis is inflammation of hair follicles on the scalp resulting from bacterial or fungal infection. It represents one of the most common infectious scalp conditions and typically responds well to appropriate topical or systemic antimicrobial therapy. Predisposing factors include poor scalp hygiene, hair trauma, and immunosuppression.

Bacterial Folliculitis

Bacterial folliculitis, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus, presents with follicular pustules, erythema, and pruritus. Superficial folliculitis involves only the follicular infundibulum, while deep folliculitis extends to deeper hair structures. Risk factors include follicular occlusion from hair care products, shaving trauma, and immunosuppression.

Fungal Folliculitis

Fungal folliculitis commonly results from Candida species, dermatophytes, or Malassezia infection. Candida folliculitis typically occurs in immunocompromised patients or those with prolonged antibiotic use. Dermatophyte folliculitis presents with follicular papules and pustules, sometimes with kerions (severe inflammatory responses). Malassezia folliculitis causes follicular papules and pustules, often pruritic.

Clinical Presentation

Folliculitis presents with:

  • Follicular pustules or papules
  • Erythema surrounding affected follicles
  • Pruritus or tenderness
  • Scale or crust formation
  • Possible permanent scarring if severe or recurrent
  • Distribution may be localized or diffuse

Diagnostic Approach

Clinical diagnosis is often sufficient. Culture from pustule contents or bacterial/fungal culture aids organism identification and antimicrobial sensitivity testing. KOH preparation or fungal culture differentiates fungal from bacterial infection.

Bacterial Folliculitis Treatment

Topical antibiotics (clindamycin, erythromycin, mupirocin) treat superficial folliculitis. Oral antibiotics (cephalexin, cloxacillin) or doxycycline benefit deep folliculitis or extensive involvement. Management of predisposing factors including hair trauma avoidance and improved scalp hygiene prevents recurrence.

Fungal Folliculitis Treatment

Topical antifungals (ketoconazole, clotrimazole) treat localized Malassezia or candidal folliculitis. Systemic antifungals (itraconazole, terbinafine) address dermatophyte or extensive fungal infection. Treatment duration depends on organism and disease severity.

Prevention and Prognosis

Most cases resolve with appropriate treatment. Recurrence may occur with continued exposure to predisposing factors. Prevention through improved scalp hygiene, gentle hair care, and management of underlying conditions aids long-term control.
meta_description: Scalp folliculitis bacterial and fungal causes, Staphylococcus aureus, topical and oral antibiotics, antifungal treatment.

title: Dissecting Cellulitis of the Scalp
content:
Overview

Dissecting cellulitis of the scalp, also known as perifolliculitis capitis abscedens et suffodiens, is a rare chronic suppurative condition of the scalp characterized by purulent infection, sinus tract formation, and eventual scarring alopecia. It predominantly affects African-American males and typically manifests in young to middle-aged adults. The condition is often refractory to treatment and carries significant morbidity including extensive scarring.

Pathophysiology

The pathogenesis remains incompletely understood. Current evidence suggests primary follicular obstruction with secondary bacterial infection, though the underlying cause of follicular dysfunction is unclear. Genetic predisposition, bacterial colonization (particularly Staphylococcus aureus), and follicular abnormalities likely contribute. The condition is not truly infectious, as antibiotics alone rarely produce sustained remission.

Clinical Presentation

Dissecting cellulitis presents with:

  • Recurrent pustules, nodules, and abscesses on the scalp
  • Sinus tract formation with purulent drainage
  • Scarring alopecia from repeated inflammation and drainage
  • Possible systemic symptoms including fever and malaise during flares
  • Intense pruritus and pain
  • Progressive scarring and disfiguring alopecia
  • Involvement typically begins on the vertex or occiput

Associated Conditions

Dissecting cellulitis may occur in association with acne conglobata and hidradenitis suppurativa as part of the follicular occlusion tetrad. Bacterial superinfection is common and complicates management.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is clinical, supported by characteristic sinus tract formation and recurrent pustulation. Bacterial culture may identify colonizing organisms. Biopsy demonstrates suppurative inflammation with follicular obstruction and possible asteroid bodies. Imaging may reveal sinus tract extent.

Medical Management

Systemic antibiotics targeting Staphylococcus aureus (cloxacillin, clindamycin) reduce bacterial colonization and pustulation, though relapse commonly occurs after discontinuation. Oral retinoids (isotretinoin) have shown benefit in some cases. Systemic corticosteroids address inflammatory component. JAK inhibitors represent emerging therapy.

Surgical Management

Extensive scarring often requires surgical excision of affected scalp with grafting. This definitive treatment removes the diseased tissue but carries risk of recurrence at graft margins and significant cosmetic implications.

Prognosis

Dissecting cellulitis is chronic and often difficult to treat. Early aggressive intervention may prevent extensive scarring. Many patients require combination medical and surgical approaches. Recurrence is common even after successful treatment.
meta_description: Dissecting cellulitis scalp suppurative infection, sinus tracts, Staphylococcal colonization, antibiotics and surgical management.

title: Trichotillomania: Hair Pulling Disorder
content:
Overview

Trichotillomania, or hair-pulling disorder, is a body-focused repetitive behavior characterized by recurrent hair pulling resulting in noticeable hair loss and distress. It is classified as an obsessive-compulsive and related disorder and affects approximately 1-2% of the population, with higher prevalence in children and adolescents. The condition often begins in childhood and may persist into adulthood.

Pathophysiology

Trichotillomania results from complex interactions between genetic predisposition, neurobiological factors (particularly serotonin and glutamate dysfunction), psychological stress, and environmental triggers. Some individuals pull hair as a tension-reduction mechanism (automatic pulling), while others pull in response to anxiety or perfectionism concerns (focused pulling).

Clinical Presentation

Typical features include:

  • Recurrent hair pulling from scalp, eyebrows, eyelashes, or body
  • Noticeable hair loss with visible bald patches or thinning
  • Short, broken hairs of variable length at pull site margins
  • Non-scarring alopecia with preserved hair follicles
  • Possible trichobezoar (hairball) formation with gastrointestinal symptoms
  • Associated anxiety, depression, or stress
  • Shame, embarrassment, or distress regarding appearance
  • Attempted hair-pulling cessation with recurrent relapse

Clinical Subtypes

Automatic pulling occurs unconsciously, often during focus on other activities, while focused pulling is intentional response to emotional distress. Mixed presentations are common. Some patients restrict pulling to specific areas while others pull from multiple sites.

Diagnostic Criteria

Diagnosis requires recurrent hair pulling resulting in noticeable hair loss, repeated attempts to decrease or stop pulling, and significant distress or functional impairment. Differentiation from alopecia areata and other causes of hair loss is important.

Psychological Assessment

Comprehensive psychological evaluation assesses severity, triggers, associated psychiatric conditions (obsessive-compulsive disorder, anxiety, depression), and impact on quality of life. Cognitive-behavioral factors including perfectionism and frustration tolerance are relevant.

Treatment Approach

Cognitive-behavioral therapy, particularly habit reversal training and comprehensive behavioral (ComB) model, demonstrates efficacy. Pharmacotherapy with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) or N-acetylcysteine provides supplementary benefit. Acceptance and commitment therapy offers alternative psychological approach. Family involvement in treatment enhances outcomes, particularly in children.

Prognosis

Early intervention improves outcomes. With appropriate treatment, many patients achieve significant improvement in pulling behavior and associated distress. Some individuals experience chronic relapsing course requiring ongoing management.
meta_description: Trichotillomania hair-pulling disorder, body-focused repetitive behavior, habit reversal training, SSRIs, CBT therapy.

title: Minoxidil for Hair Loss: Efficacy and Application
content:
Overview

Minoxidil is the most widely used topical medication for hair loss treatment and is FDA-approved for androgenetic alopecia in both men and women. Originally developed as an oral antihypertensive agent, its effectiveness for promoting hair growth was discovered incidentally. Topical minoxidil has demonstrated efficacy in promoting hair regrowth and slowing hair loss progression across multiple alopecia types.

Mechanism of Action

Minoxidil's exact mechanism for promoting hair growth remains incompletely elucidated, though evidence supports multiple pathways including increased blood flow to hair follicles, prolongation of anagen (growth) phase, increased size of hair follicles, and enhancement of potassium channel opening. It may also possess anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic properties relevant to scarring alopecias.

Formulations and Strengths

Topical minoxidil is available in 2% and 5% concentrations. The 5% solution demonstrates superior efficacy compared to 2%, particularly in men. Formulations include solutions, foams, and gels. Solutions penetrate hair-covered scalp more effectively, while foams and gels offer superior cosmetic acceptability and reduced greasy appearance.

Efficacy Profile

Clinical trials demonstrate that approximately 40-50% of men treated with minoxidil experience moderate to significant hair regrowth, while 33-40% experience stabilization of hair loss. Women show similar or slightly superior response rates. Improvement typically requires 3-6 months of consistent application. Maximum benefit occurs at 12 months, with potential for continued improvement at 24 months.

Application and Dosing

Standard application involves 1 mL of solution or gram of foam applied to dry scalp twice daily. Scalp must be completely dry before application. Users should wait 4 hours before shampooing. Consistent adherence is essential for maintaining benefits.

Adverse Effects

Local side effects include pruritus, erythema, irritant or allergic contact dermatitis, and headache. Systemic absorption is minimal at recommended topical doses, making systemic adverse effects uncommon. Initial shedding during first 2-3 months may occur as follicles cycle into new growth phases, then stabilize and improve.

Contraindications and Precautions

Minoxidil is contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension and cardiac disease. Pregnancy category C requires careful risk-benefit assessment. Simultaneous use with other topical scalp medications may reduce efficacy or cause irritation.

Duration and Maintenance

Minoxidil benefits require ongoing application. Hair loss resumes within months to a year of discontinuation. Long-term use demonstrates continued safety and maintained efficacy. Cost and duration of application represent practical limitations for some patients.
meta_description: Minoxidil topical treatment for hair loss, 2% vs 5% formulations, application twice daily, 3-6 months for results.

title: Finasteride for Hair Loss: 5-Alpha Reductase Inhibition
content:
Overview

Finasteride is an oral 5-alpha reductase inhibitor FDA-approved for male pattern baldness treatment. By inhibiting conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT), finasteride addresses the hormonal basis of androgenetic alopecia. It represents the first and most extensively studied oral therapy for hair loss and demonstrates substantial efficacy in slowing and reversing androgenetic alopecia progression.

Mechanism of Action

Finasteride inhibits 5-alpha reductase, the enzyme converting testosterone to DHT. By reducing scalp DHT levels by approximately 70%, follicular miniaturization is halted and potentially reversed. This hormone-specific mechanism makes finasteride particularly effective for DHT-dependent alopecia.

Dosing and Administration

The standard dose for hair loss treatment is 1 mg daily, taken orally without regard to meals. This lower dose (Propecia) was specifically approved for hair loss, distinct from the 5 mg dose (Proscar) used for benign prostatic hyperplasia. Consistent daily administration is necessary, with therapeutic effects requiring 3-6 months to become apparent.

Efficacy in Men

Clinical trials demonstrate that approximately 66% of men using finasteride experience hair regrowth, while 48% achieve dense hair regrowth. In non-responders, finasteride effectively slows or stabilizes hair loss in up to 88% of users. Longer treatment duration (>2 years) yields superior outcomes. Efficacy is greatest in younger men with early-stage baldness.

Use in Women

Finasteride is not FDA-approved for female pattern hair loss and is relatively contraindicated due to pregnancy risks and theoretical concerns regarding decreased 5-alpha reductase in women. However, postmenopausal women may benefit from finasteride for androgenetic alopecia, particularly when elevated androgens are present. Pregnancy category X precludes use in premenopausal women due to potential feminization of male fetuses.

Adverse Effects

Sexual dysfunction occurs in 1-3% of users and may include decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, or ejaculation disorders. These effects are generally reversible upon discontinuation, though delayed resolution occurs in some patients. Gynecomastia (breast enlargement) is rare. Systemic absorption is minimal with standard 1 mg dosing.

Monitoring and Long-Term Outcomes

Long-term studies (5-10 years) demonstrate sustained efficacy and safety with continuous finasteride use. Discontinuation results in hair loss resumption, necessitating indefinite treatment for maintained benefit. Combination with minoxidil enhances efficacy compared to either agent alone.

Drug Interactions and Monitoring

Finasteride has minimal drug interactions. Baseline and periodic PSA (prostate-specific antigen) assessment should be considered, as finasteride may lower PSA levels in men receiving prostate cancer screening. Overall mortality and prostate cancer incidence studies show reassuring safety profiles.
meta_description: Finasteride 1 mg oral tablet for male pattern baldness, 5-alpha reductase inhibitor, DHT reduction, sexual side effects.

title: Spironolactone for Hair Loss: Antiandrogen Therapy
content:
Overview

Spironolactone is an oral potassium-sparing diuretic with antiandrogen properties used off-label for female pattern hair loss and other androgen-dependent alopecias in women. While not FDA-approved specifically for hair loss, spironolactone effectively reduces circulating androgens and blocks androgen receptor signaling, making it particularly valuable for women with hyperandrogenism or those intolerant of other treatments.

Mechanism of Action

Spironolactone functions as a non-selective androgen receptor antagonist and inhibits ovarian and adrenal androgen synthesis. By blocking DHT effects at the hair follicle receptor level, it prevents androgen-induced miniaturization. This dual action makes spironolactone effective even when DHT levels are not significantly elevated.

Dosing and Administration

Hair loss treatment typically employs 100-200 mg daily in divided doses. Most patients receive 50-100 mg twice daily. Therapeutic effects require 3-6 months to become apparent, with maximum benefit at 12 months. Combination with oral contraceptives or other hormonal therapy enhances efficacy and provides contraceptive benefit.

Efficacy in Female Pattern Hair Loss

Clinical experience demonstrates effectiveness in slowing hair loss and promoting regrowth in 50-75% of women, particularly those with documented hyperandrogenism or PCOS. Response rates appear superior in younger women with higher baseline androgens. Efficacy comparable to or exceeding minoxidil has been reported in some series.

Adverse Effects

Common side effects include irregular menstrual cycles, breast tenderness, decreased libido, and fatigue. Hyperkalemia represents a significant concern, particularly in patients with renal impairment or concurrent ACE inhibitor/ARB use. Regular monitoring of serum potassium and renal function is essential.

Monitoring Requirements

Baseline potassium, creatinine, and blood pressure assessment should be obtained. Periodic monitoring of potassium and renal function (every 3-6 months initially, then annually) is essential. Contraindications include pregnancy, renal failure, and uncontrolled hyperkalemia. Drug interactions with other agents affecting potassium homeostasis require careful consideration.

Contraindications in Pregnancy

Spironolactone is contraindicated in pregnancy due to potential feminization of male fetuses. Reliable contraception is essential for women of childbearing potential. This limitation precludes use in premenopausal women not using contraception.

Combination Therapy

Combined spironolactone and minoxidil or finasteride provides superior outcomes compared to monotherapy in many patients. Oral contraceptives combined with spironolactone offer both hair loss treatment and contraceptive benefits.
meta_description: Spironolactone antiandrogen therapy for female hair loss, 100-200 mg daily, potassium monitoring, contraindicated in pregnancy.

title: Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP) for Hair Restoration
content:
Overview

Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) therapy is an emerging regenerative medicine approach for hair loss wherein autologous platelet concentrate is injected into the scalp to stimulate hair follicle growth and improve hair density. While preliminary evidence shows promise, larger controlled trials are needed to establish efficacy definitively. PRP represents an option for patients seeking adjunctive or alternative treatment approaches.

Mechanism of Action

PRP contains concentrated platelets with alpha-granules releasing growth factors including platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), and fibroblast growth factor (FGF). These growth factors stimulate angiogenesis, reduce inflammation, promote follicle activation, and extend anagen phase duration.

Preparation Methods

PRP is obtained through centrifugation of autologous whole blood to concentrate platelets. Various commercial systems and protocols exist, producing variable platelet concentrations (3-8 fold enrichment typical) and growth factor compositions. Standardization of preparation methods remains a significant challenge in PRP therapy.

Administration Technique

PRP is injected into the dermis overlying affected hair follicles using fine needle injection or dermaroller-assisted administration. Treatment typically involves 2-4 mL injected into multiple sites throughout affected scalp. Most protocols recommend monthly injections for 3 months, with maintenance injections every 3-6 months.

Clinical Evidence

Published series demonstrate modest improvements in hair density and shaft diameter in treated patients. However, many studies suffer from small sample sizes, lack of control groups, and variable outcome measures. Published evidence overall supports potential benefit, though larger controlled trials are needed.

Efficacy in Hair Loss Types

PRP may benefit androgenetic alopecia, alopecia areata, and telogen effluvium. Data specifically addressing cicatricial alopecias remain limited. Combination with topical minoxidil or finasteride may enhance outcomes compared to PRP monotherapy.

Adverse Effects and Safety

PRP therapy carries minimal systemic adverse effects given autologous nature. Local adverse effects include injection site pain, erythema, and transient swelling. No serious adverse effects have been documented in published literature. Risk profile appears favorable.

Cost and Accessibility

PRP therapy remains expensive (typically $500-1500 per treatment session), with costs generally not covered by insurance. This cost barrier limits accessibility for many patients. Standardization of protocols and larger controlled trials would strengthen clinical evidence and potentially improve insurance coverage.

Current Status and Future Directions

PRP represents an emerging but unproven therapy for hair loss. While preliminary results are encouraging, robust clinical evidence supporting routine use remains limited. Further research with standardized protocols, larger sample sizes, and longer follow-up would clarify PRP's role in hair loss management.
meta_description: Platelet-rich plasma hair restoration, growth factors, monthly injections, emerging evidence for androgenetic alopecia.

title: Hair Transplantation: FUE vs FUT Techniques
content:
Overview

Hair transplantation is the most effective definitive treatment for androgenetic alopecia and other causes of permanent alopecia. Two primary techniques—follicular unit excision (FUE) and follicular unit transplantation (FUT)—offer surgical hair restoration with varying advantages and limitations. Selection between techniques depends on hair loss extent, donor area characteristics, patient preferences, and surgeon expertise.

Follicular Unit Transplantation (FUT)

FUT, also known as strip harvesting, involves removal of a linear strip of scalp containing hair follicles from the occipital donor area. The strip is microscopically dissected into individual follicular units containing 1-4 hairs. Advantages include more rapid harvesting and lower cost. Disadvantages include a linear scar at the donor site and potential for shock loss. FUT remains excellent for patients requiring large numbers of grafts.

Follicular Unit Excision (FUE)

FUE involves direct extraction of individual follicular units from the scalp using specialized motorized or manual punches. This technique preserves more of the surrounding tissue and eliminates the linear donor scar. Advantages include improved cosmetic outcomes, lack of linear scar, and ability to harvest from diverse donor areas. Disadvantages include longer procedure time, higher cost, and potential for visible punch marks if not meticulously performed.

Graft Survival and Density

Both FUE and FUT achieve similar graft survival rates (85-95%) when performed by experienced surgeons. Final hair density depends on graft numbers and distribution pattern. Realistic expectations regarding coverage improvement rather than complete baldness restoration are important for patient satisfaction.

Donor Area Considerations

Permanent hair in the occipital and temporal scalp demonstrates resistance to DHT miniaturization, making it ideal donor tissue. Sufficient donor hair density and quantity determine feasible graft numbers. Assessment of donor characteristics and lifetime supply guides candidacy and extent of transplantation.

Recipient Area Preparation

Recipient site creation establishes precise placement and angulation for natural appearance. Hair direction, density, and hairline configuration must be carefully planned. Angulation and direction particularly influence frontal hairline aesthetic outcomes.

Procedure Duration and Sessions

Transplantation procedures typically require 4-8 hours depending on graft number. Many patients require multiple sessions to achieve desired density and coverage. Optimal spacing between sessions (minimum 3 months) allows adequate healing and assessment.

Post-Operative Course

Grafted hair typically sheds within 2-3 weeks, with new growth resuming at 3-4 months. Maximum cosmetic improvement requires 12-18 months as transplanted hairs complete growth cycles. Temporary shedding of pre-existing native hair (shock loss) may occur but typically recovers over months.

Complications

Infection, bleeding, scarring, and poor hair growth represent potential complications. Natural appearance failure relates to improper graft placement or poor recipient site design rather than graft quality. Infection rates remain low with appropriate perioperative care.

Long-Term Outcomes

Transplanted hair demonstrates excellent long-term survival. Most patients maintain stable hair growth at 5-10 year follow-up. Continued hair loss in non-transplanted areas may create density discrepancies requiring camouflage with medical therapy or additional surgery.
meta_description: Hair transplantation FUE vs FUT techniques, graft survival, donor sites, hairline design, 12-18 month results.

title: Low-Level Laser Therapy for Hair Growth
content:
Overview

Low-level laser therapy (LLLT), also termed red light therapy or photobiomodulation, uses specific wavelengths of light (typically 600-1100 nm) to stimulate hair follicle growth and potentially slow hair loss. While preliminary evidence suggests modest benefits, larger controlled trials are necessary to definitively establish efficacy. LLLT represents a non-invasive, low-risk option for adjunctive hair loss treatment.

Mechanism of Action

Low-level laser light penetrates scalp tissue to follicular epithelium, stimulating mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) production. This cellular energy enhancement promotes anagen phase extension, increases follicle size, and may reduce inflammation. Precise mechanisms in hair follicles remain incompletely characterized.

Treatment Parameters

Effective LLLT typically employs wavelengths of 600-1100 nm, most commonly red (650 nm) or near-infrared (700-1100 nm) light. Power densities and energy densities vary among devices. Typical treatment protocols involve 15-30 minute sessions 2-3 times weekly using dedicated laser caps, combs, or panels.

Devices and Applications

Various FDA-cleared LLLT devices are available for home use including laser caps, handheld combs, and stationary panels. Established manufacturers have conducted clinical trials supporting efficacy for androgenetic alopecia. Device cost ranges from $500-3000, with variation based on wavelength, power, and design.

Clinical Evidence

Published studies demonstrate modest improvements in hair count and density in approximately 60% of patients receiving LLLT. Superiority to placebo has been demonstrated in some trials, though effect sizes remain modest. Combination with topical minoxidil may enhance outcomes compared to LLLT monotherapy.

Efficacy in Different Hair Loss Types

LLLT demonstrates efficacy in androgenetic alopecia with stronger evidence in male pattern baldness than female pattern loss. Evidence for LLLT benefit in alopecia areata and other non-androgenetic alopecias remains more limited. Long-term efficacy data >24 months are sparse.

Adverse Effects

LLLT carries minimal adverse effect profile. Transient erythema, pruritus, and folliculitis have been reported. No serious adverse effects documented in literature. This excellent safety profile makes LLLT appropriate for long-term use.

Limitations and Considerations

LLLT efficacy remains modest compared to established medical therapies. Significant patient investment in equipment and ongoing time commitment required. Variable device quality and lack of standardization across manufacturers complicate interpretation of evidence. Insurance coverage typically absent.

Current Role in Hair Loss Management

LLLT represents a reasonable adjunctive option for motivated patients seeking non-medical approaches. Combination with topical or oral therapies may provide superior outcomes. Evidence base would benefit from larger prospective controlled trials with standardized protocols and longer follow-up.
meta_description: Low-level laser therapy photobiomodulation for hair growth, red light 650 nm wavelength, modest efficacy in androgenetic alopecia.

title: Scalp Biopsy: Technique and Diagnostic Applications
content:
Overview

Scalp biopsy is a valuable diagnostic tool for evaluating certain types of alopecia, particularly scarring alopecias and inflammatory scalp conditions where clinical diagnosis is uncertain. The procedure provides histopathologic information that guides treatment decisions and prognosis assessment. Understanding indications, techniques, and interpretation is essential for optimal diagnostic outcomes.

Indications for Scalp Biopsy

Biopsy is indicated in suspected scarring alopecias (lichen planopilaris, frontal fibrosing alopecia, central centrifugal cicatricial alopecia) to confirm diagnosis and assess disease stage. Inflammatory scalp conditions with atypical presentation may require biopsy for definitive diagnosis. Biopsy is generally not necessary for typical androgenetic alopecia or alopecia areata with characteristic clinical features.

Biopsy Technique

A 4 mm punch biopsy is typically obtained from the affected scalp area at the active disease margin when present. The specimen should include skin and subcutaneous tissue to full depth for optimal assessment. Horizontal (cross-sectional) orientation is preferred for dermatitis assessment, while vertical (longitudinal) orientation better evaluates hair follicles. Two biopsies may be obtained for optimal diagnostic assessment in some cases.

Specimen Handling

Specimens are fixed in 10% formalin and submitted for routine histopathologic examination. Immunohistochemistry with T-cell markers (CD4, CD8) may be helpful in some cases to characterize inflammatory infiltrate. The specimen should include multiple follicles at varying depths to adequately assess follicular changes.

Histopathologic Findings in Scarring Alopecias

Scarring alopecias demonstrate permanent loss of hair follicles with replacement fibrosis. Primary scarring alopecias (lichen planopilaris, frontal fibrosing alopecia) show characteristic lymphocytic inflammation with follicular destruction. Secondary scarring alopecias result from external destructive processes. Loss of sebaceous glands and arrector pili muscles indicates true scarring.

Histopathologic Findings in Non-Scarring Alopecias

Non-scarring alopecias preserve hair follicle structures with variable changes in follicular distribution and cycle stage. Alopecia areata demonstrates lymphocytic infiltration around anagen follicles with preserved follicular architecture. Telogen effluvium shows increased telogen phase hairs. Androgenetic alopecia reveals miniaturized follicles with preserved sebaceous glands.

Diagnostic Yield

Scalp biopsy diagnostic yield is highest in suspected scarring alopecias where histology directly establishes diagnosis. Yield is lower in suspected non-scarring alopecias where clinical diagnosis is usually sufficient. Disease stage assessment (early inflammatory versus chronic scarring) influences treatment selection and prognosis.

Limitations and Considerations

Biopsy provides focal assessment of usually diffuse process; multiple biopsies may be needed. Some conditions require specific clinical context for proper interpretation. Early disease may show minimal histologic changes despite clinical activity. Biopsy site represents permanent small scar, requiring cosmetic consideration.

Interpretation and Clinical Correlation

Histopathologic interpretation requires correlation with clinical presentation and patient history. Dermatologist expertise in scalp histopathology ensures accurate diagnosis and appropriate therapeutic recommendations based on biopsy findings.
meta_description: Scalp biopsy 4 mm punch technique, histopathology scarring alopecias, lichen planopilaris, diagnostic indications.

title: Hair Shaft Disorders: Trichorrhexis Nodosa
content:
Overview

Trichorrhexis nodosa is a structural hair shaft disorder characterized by nodal defects where the hair shaft appears to split or fracture. The condition may be congenital (inherited) or acquired (caused by trauma, chemical exposure, or underlying disease). While trichorrhexis nodosa does not cause permanent alopecia, it significantly impacts hair appearance and cosmetic concerns.

Pathophysiology

In trichorrhexis nodosa, cortical cells of the hair shaft degenerate, creating weak points susceptible to fracturing. The result is characteristic nodose (node-like) appearance with hair shaft narrowing at nodal sites. Congenital forms result from structural hair protein abnormalities, while acquired forms result from external damage.

Clinical Presentation

Patients present with:

  • Short, fragile hair with premature breakage
  • Characteristic nodular appearance on microscopy (resembling a broomstick)
  • Hair that fractures easily with minimal manipulation or grooming
  • Hair appearance of progressive shortening despite normal growth
  • Possible associated scaling or dermatitis
  • History of chemical treatments, heat styling, or physical trauma in acquired forms

Congenital Trichorrhexis Nodosa

Congenital forms are rare, inherited disorders of hair protein structure. Argininosuccinic aciduria, a metabolic disorder, associates with congenital trichorrhexis nodosa. These forms typically present in infancy with short, brittle hair and associated systemic features.

Acquired Trichorrhexis Nodosa

Acquired forms result from extrinsic damage including alkaline exposure, mechanical trauma from rough handling, excessive heat styling, and chemical treatments (permanent waves, straightening, bleaching). Nutritional deficiencies, internal disease (kidney disease, nutritional deficiency), or certain medications may predispose to acquired trichorrhexis nodosa.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is established through hair microscopy revealing characteristic nodal defects with hair shaft narrowing. Light microscopy or scanning electron microscopy visualizes the distinctive appearance. Associated dermatitis or scales may be visible on scalp examination.

Management Principles

Treatment of acquired trichorrhexis nodosa focuses on eliminating the inciting factor. Cessation of harsh chemical treatments, excessive heat styling, and rough grooming techniques is essential. Gentle hair handling with wide-toothed combs and minimal manipulations supports hair preservation. Nutritional supplementation may benefit deficient patients.

Prognosis and Outcomes

Acquired trichorrhexis nodosa resolves as damaged hair is shed and new hair growth occurs after inciting factor removal. Complete hair recovery requires several months as new undamaged hair emerges. Congenital forms are permanent but manageable with protective hair care practices.
meta_description: Trichorrhexis nodosa hair shaft splitting, nodal defects, acquired vs congenital forms, chemical and thermal damage.

title: Alopecia in Children: Diagnosis and Management
content:
Overview

Hair loss in children presents diagnostic and management challenges differing significantly from adult alopecia. Alopecia areata is the most common cause of hair loss in children, though tinea capitis, telogen effluvium, and traction alopecia must also be considered. Age-appropriate evaluation and treatment approaches optimize outcomes and minimize psychological impact.

Alopecia Areata in Children

Childhood-onset alopecia areata (peak age 4-8 years) represents approximately 25% of all alopecia areata cases. Children often present with multiple alopecic patches with minimal awareness of hair loss, contrasting with adult presentation. Parental concern typically prompts evaluation. Prognosis in childhood-onset disease shows higher spontaneous remission rates compared to adult-onset disease, with approximately 50-70% experiencing regrowth within 1 year.

Tinea Capitis (Scalp Ringworm)

Fungal scalp infection is common in children and must be differentiated from alopecia areata. Tinea capitis presents with inflammation, scale, and hair breakage rather than clean hair loss. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation or fungal culture confirms diagnosis. Systemic antifungal therapy (griseofulvin, terbinafine) is required for tinea capitis treatment.

Telogen Effluvium in Children

Childhood telogen effluvium frequently follows acute illness, fever, surgery, or emotional stress. Hair shedding appears 2-4 weeks after the triggering event. Clinical features mirror adult telogen effluvium with diffuse shedding, normal scalp appearance, and positive hair pull test. Most cases resolve spontaneously within months without treatment.

Traction Alopecia in Children

Tight hairstyling practices (braids, weaves, high buns, extensions) cause traction alopecia particularly in African-American children. Early recognition and modification of hairstyle practices prevent permanent scarring. Hair styling counseling directing parents toward looser, protective styles is paramount.

Trichotillomania in Children

Hair pulling disorder occurs in school-age children and may follow stressful events or represent anxiety-related behavior. Psychological evaluation and behavioral interventions are essential. Parental awareness of causative stressors aids management. Most children respond well to behavioral approaches without medication.

Androgenetic Alopecia in Children

Prepubertal androgenetic alopecia is rare, warranting evaluation for endocrine abnormality. Postpubertal androgenetic alopecia in adolescents represents early-onset disease with implications for long-term progression. Medical therapy discussion should address age-appropriate treatments and long-term commitment.

Diagnostic Approach

Comprehensive history including onset, progression pattern, triggering factors, and family history aids diagnosis. Scalp examination with attention to inflammation, scale, follicular openings, and hair pull test informs differential diagnosis. Fungal culture or KOH preparation excludes tinea capitis. Biopsy is rarely needed in children with typical presentation.

Treatment Considerations

Medical treatment of childhood alopecia aims to minimize child distress while avoiding unnecessary medication in conditions with high spontaneous remission rates. Topical corticosteroids and intralesional injections can be performed with local anesthesia (cream or needle-free jet injection) for alopecia areata. Reassurance regarding likely recovery in many childhood alopecias is important.

Psychological Impact

Hair loss significantly affects children's self-esteem and social interaction. Age-appropriate counseling addressing cosmetic concerns (wigs, hats) and psychological support are important treatment components. School involvement may be necessary to address peer interactions and bullying concerns.
meta_description: Childhood alopecia areata, tinea capitis, telogen effluvium, traction alopecia, diagnostic approach in children.

title: Postpartum Hair Loss: Telogen Effluvium After Pregnancy
content:
Overview

Postpartum telogen effluvium is a form of diffuse hair shedding occurring within 2-4 months after delivery, affecting up to 40-50% of postpartum women. This non-scarring alopecia results from synchronized shift of hair follicles into telogen phase triggered by postpartum hormonal changes. Most cases resolve spontaneously within 6-12 months with reassurance and supportive care.

Pathophysiology

Pregnancy maintains elevated estrogen levels that extend anagen (growth) phase duration, resulting in increased hair density and decreased shedding. Postpartum estrogen decline triggers synchronized telogen conversion of multiple follicles, resulting in increased shedding approximately 2-4 months postpartum as telogen hairs enter exogen (shedding) phase.

Clinical Features

Postpartum telogen effluvium typically presents with:

  • Sudden onset of increased hair shedding noticed during shampooing or brushing
  • Diffuse hair thinning most notable at the scalp margin and parting
  • Timeline of onset 2-4 months postpartum
  • Preserved scalp appearance without inflammation or scarring
  • Positive hair pull test with telogen hairs obtained
  • Associated anxiety regarding hair loss extent and permanence

Risk Factors

While postpartum telogen effluvium occurs across diverse populations, certain factors increase risk: postpartum anemia, nutritional deficiency, iron depletion, stress, thyroid dysfunction, and inadequate recovery. These modifiable factors present treatment opportunities.

Differential Diagnosis

Postpartum thyroiditis and other thyroid disorders may present concurrently with postpartum telogen effluvium, necessitating thyroid function assessment. Alopecia areata onset postpartum must be differentiated from telogen effluvium by patch formation and typical clinical features. Iron deficiency anemia frequently accompanies postpartum hair loss and requires management.

Diagnostic Approach

Diagnosis is primarily clinical based on postpartum timing, diffuse shedding pattern, and positive hair pull test. Laboratory evaluation including complete blood count, iron studies, thyroid function, and other parameters based on clinical suspicion aids identification of modifiable contributing factors.

Natural History and Prognosis

Most postpartum telogen effluvium resolves spontaneously within 6-12 months as the hair growth cycle normalizes. Some women experience persistent shedding or chronic telogen effluvium extending beyond one year. Complete hair recovery is expected in majority of cases without treatment.

Management Approach

Reassurance regarding the benign and self-limited nature of postpartum telogen effluvium is central to management. Nutritional optimization including iron supplementation if deficient, protein sufficiency, and B-vitamin supplementation may support hair recovery. Gentle hair handling and avoidance of tight hairstyles minimizes mechanical shedding. Topical minoxidil may provide psychological benefit though not universally necessary.

Breastfeeding Considerations

Breastfeeding does not exacerbate postpartum hair loss, and continuation is recommended. Nutritional requirements for breastfeeding are met through adequate intake, and no medications routinely used for telogen effluvium present breastfeeding contraindications.
meta_description: Postpartum telogen effluvium 2-4 months after delivery, estrogen-related shedding, iron supplementation, reassurance.

title: Thyroid Disease and Hair Loss
content:
Overview

Thyroid dysfunction significantly impacts hair health and represents an important reversible cause of hair loss. Both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism associate with alopecia, most commonly as telogen effluvium. Recognition and treatment of underlying thyroid disease often improves hair growth. Hair loss may represent the initial manifestation of previously unrecognized thyroid dysfunction.

Hypothyroidism and Hair Loss

Hypothyroidism reduces metabolic rate and thyroid hormone-dependent protein synthesis, affecting hair follicle function. Thyroid hormones are essential for normal hair growth, and deficiency impairs anagen phase duration and follicle metabolism. Hypothyroidism associates with telogen effluvium, diffuse hair thinning, dry brittle hair, and loss of outer eyebrow hairs.

Hyperthyroidism and Hair Loss

Excessive thyroid hormone increases metabolic demands on hair follicles beyond their capacity, leading to telogen effluvium. Thyrotoxicosis stresses follicular metabolism and triggers conversion to telogen phase. Hair loss with hyperthyroidism may be severe but typically resolves with thyroid hormone normalization.

Hair Loss Presentation

Thyroid-related hair loss typically manifests as diffuse telogen effluvium rather than localized alopecia. Onset follows thyroid disease development by several months. Associated features including fatigue, weight changes, skin changes, temperature intolerance, and menstrual irregularities aid diagnosis. Hair loss may represent the patient's primary presenting complaint.

Diagnostic Approach

Thyroid function testing (TSH, free T4) should be performed in all patients presenting with telogen effluvium or unexplained diffuse hair loss. Thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin (TSI) and thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies identify autoimmune thyroiditis. Early identification enables prompt treatment initiation.

Thyroid Treatment and Hair Recovery

Hair loss improves with thyroid hormone replacement in hypothyroidism or antithyroid therapy in hyperthyroidism. Optimal TSH control is essential for hair recovery. Some patients demonstrate persistent telogen effluvium initially despite thyroid normalization, as follicles complete telogen phase. Hair recovery typically occurs 3-6 months after thyroid normalization.

Postpartum Thyroiditis

Postpartum thyroiditis occurs in 5-10% of postpartum women and often presents as transient hypothyroidism 2-8 weeks postpartum. The temporal overlap with postpartum telogen effluvium can confound diagnosis. Thyroid screening is indicated in postpartum women with excessive hair shedding to exclude concurrent thyroiditis.

Hair Loss Duration After Treatment

Following thyroid hormone normalization, continued telogen effluvium lasting 2-6 months is typical as shedding hair completes exogen phase and new growth emerges. Patient counseling regarding this lag between thyroid normalization and hair recovery improves compliance and prevents unnecessary therapy escalation.

Other Thyroid-Related Concerns

Alopecia areata associates with Graves' disease and Hashimoto's thyroiditis due to shared autoimmune pathophysiology. Some thyroid medications may associate with alopecia (propranolol), though causative relationship is debated. Iodine excess or deficiency affects hair growth through effects on thyroid function.
meta_description: Thyroid disease telogen effluvium, hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism hair loss, TSH normalization for recovery.

title: Iron Deficiency and Hair Loss
content:
Overview

Iron deficiency represents an important modifiable cause of hair loss affecting both ferritin-dependent telogen effluvium and potentially androgenetic alopecia. Iron is essential for hemoglobin synthesis, myoglobin function, and iron-containing enzymes critical to hair follicle metabolism. Recognition and treatment of iron deficiency often improves hair growth, making this a particularly rewarding treatable etiology.

Pathophysiology

Hair follicles are metabolically active tissues requiring substantial ATP production for growth.
